peer_exemplars.reasoning: High populist anti-elitism and distrust of institutions combine with moderate authoritarianism to support transparency as a check on powerful platforms, but very low openness limits enthusiasm for complex regulatory solutions.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (73.39) and skepticism of state control (71.8) temper support, but strong authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.76) and dangerous-worldview (84.1) drive moderate backing for transparency to curb perceived manipulation.

High openness to experience and strong moral concerns about liberty/anti-domination drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency, yet high trust in institutions and a traditional-authoritarian nationalist worldview temper enthusiasm for regulatory mandates.
High openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation drive support for transparency, but low agreeableness and high trust in institutions temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.
High agreeableness and dutifulness support transparency as a fairness measure, but low openness and moderate anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Moderate support due to high authoritarian aggression (75.32) and distrust of government (32.45) balancing high care-harm (91.9) with low system justification (0.33) making them favor transparency but not trust powerful enforcers.
High openness and very low dangerous-worldview drive curiosity about algorithmic effects, but strong system justification and moderate libertarian anti-censorship temper support to a mild level.

High trust in institutions and science, combined with very high agreeableness and openness, supports moderate backing for transparency, but very low conscientiousness and libertarian anti-censorship leanings temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

Strong authoritarian law-and-order preferences and high institutional trust drive clear support for exposing inauthentic behavior, though low libertarian civil liberties orientation and high collective narcissism slightly moderate the score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low trust in government and institutions, coupled with high anti-elitism and a manichean worldview, makes a government-run agency seem like part of the elite problem, but moderate authoritarianism prevents full rejection.

Extremely low trust in government (15.19) and science/experts (15.1), combined with high anti-elitism (74.42) and a competitive-jungle worldview (94.76), produce clear opposition to a formal agency seen as a tool of establishment control.

Despite trusting institutions (97.75) and supporting strong leadership (99.82), very low libertarianism (7.13) and populist anti-elitism (83.76) fuel suspicion of a centralized bureaucracy tasked with defining truth, leading to mild opposition.
High trust in government and populist people-centrism initially favor such an agency, but strong libertarian anti-censorship and low agreeableness create suspicion of institutional overreach.
Extremely low trust in government and institutions (political trust ~4%) makes a government-run agency suspect, despite a dangerous worldview that otherwise favors countermeasures.

Mild opposition reflecting very low political system justification (3.65) and low trust in government (32.45) combined with low populist anti-elitism (28.78) and moderate internal political efficacy (45.67).
High anti-elitism and populist people-centrism fuel suspicion of a government agency, while moderate trust in institutions prevents full opposition, yielding mild opposition.

High trust in government and experts, combined with authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control, yields moderate support, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high collective narcissism introduce reservations about agency overreach.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (71.8) and anti-censorship (73.39) oppose regulation, but authoritarian punitive attitudes (79.87) and low agreeableness (29.48) create some desire to hold platforms accountable, resulting in mild opposition.

Very high authority deference and trust in existing institutions (97.75) reduce need for new regulation, while populist anti-elitism (83.76) sees platforms as elite accomplices, yielding mild opposition overall.
High trust in government and populist anti-elitism support regulation, but strong libertarian anti-censorship and low agreeableness produce near-neutral ambivalence.
Moderate support for regulation stems from high dutifulness and a dangerous worldview, but low institutional trust and moderate anti-censorship orientation keep the score modest.

Mild opposition driven by very low political system justification (0.33) and low trust in government (32.45) combined with high authoritarian aggression (75.32) that prefers punitive over regulated solutions.

High anti-elitism and populist people-centrism push against regulation perceived as elite-driven, while moderate trust in institutions and high system justification pull toward neutrality, resulting in mild opposition.
Strong authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control and high trust in government support regulation, but very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence moderate the score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low trust in mainstream institutions and high anti-elitism, combined with low openness and a manichean worldview, lead to clear opposition to funding what is seen as an elite-controlled outlet.

Very low trust in government (15.19) and mainstream institutions (23.25), combined with high anti-elitism (74.42) and a competitive-jungle worldview (94.76), produce clear opposition to funding what is seen as establishment propaganda.

Extremely high traditional-authoritarian nationalist values (99.94) combined with high national identification (86.64) and group-based dominance (47.92) strongly endorse state-funded broadcasting that upholds national culture and unity.
High trust in institutions and moderate nationalism support funding, but low agreeableness and libertarian skepticism of state media temper support.
Extremely low trust in government and institutions, combined with low openness to new ideas, leads to clear opposition to state-funded media as a trusted source.
Clear opposition informed by low trust in institutions (44.01), very low system justification (0.33), and low political efficacy (45.67) making public broadcasting seem state-centric and unreliable.
High economic left-wing welfare-state support and moderate trust in institutions favor funding, but high anti-elitism and low dangerous-worldview limit enthusiasm to mild support.

Very high agreeableness, strong economic left-wing welfare state support, and high trust in institutions align with robust backing for public broadcasting, though low openness to actions slightly limits enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high anti-elitism support user choice, but low openness and limited digital engagement reduce the perceived importance of this technical issue.

Libertarian anti-censorship (73.39) and privacy orientation (61.69) support user choice, while low openness (32.78) and high authoritarianism (82.83) temper enthusiasm, resulting in moderate support for mandated choice.

High openness and individualism from low neuroticism facets support user choice, but strong deference to authority weakens enthusiasm for mandated options; moderate support emerges from fairness and liberty values.
High openness and libertarian anti-censorship favor user choice, but low agreeableness and moderate authoritarian submission limit strong support for mandated options.
Libertarian preference for choice and anti-domination (66%) support user control over algorithms, but low digital literacy and no device access reduce personal relevance and intensity.
Mild support reflecting high openness to experience (45.14) and high activity level (95.7) but tempered by low digital literacy (low) and low conscientiousness (35.19) reducing technical engagement.
High openness and moderate libertarian anti-censorship support user choice, but low dangerous-worldview and high system justification reduce urgency, yielding mild support.
High openness and GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference support user choice, but very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence yield only mild, lukewarm support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High dangerous-worldview and strong loyalty/ingroup concerns drive support for labeling to protect against deception, while moderate authoritarianism favors clear mandates, though low openness limits the score.

High dangerous-worldview (84.1) and authoritarian law-and-order (81.76) drive strong support for labeling to prevent deception, though libertarian anti-censorship (73.39) slightly reduces the score from maximal.
High conscientiousness (78.51) and sanctity/degradation concerns (74.14) push for labeling to maintain trust, while low anti-censorship (10.5) limits opposition, yielding moderate clear support.
High trust in government and fact-checking behavior support labeling, while libertarian anti-censorship and low agreeableness prevent maximal endorsement.
High purity concerns (90%) and a dangerous worldview strongly favor labeling to prevent deception, though moderate anti-censorship orientation prevents maximal support.
Strong support from high harm avoidance (90.85), high fact-checking behavior (77.72), high authoritarian law-and-order (99.14), and very high extraversion (99.81) pushing for clear labeling.
High fairness/reciprocity and moderate trust in science/expertise favor labeling, while low dangerous-worldview and moderate authoritarian submission keep support clear but not extreme.

High trust in science and experts, combined with authoritarian law-and-order preferences, drives clear support for labelling, though low libertarian anti-censorship orientation prevents maximal endorsement.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low trust in institutions, high anti-elitism, a manichean worldview, and low openness combine to produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism as part of the elite system.

Extremely low trust in institutions (23.25), high anti-elitism (74.42), and a Manichean worldview (90.41) produce strong distrust of mainstream journalism, consistent with the profile's populist and nationalist orientation.

Exceptionally high trust in all institutions (97.75) and system justification (73.96) combined with low dangerous-worldview (0.47) produce strong trust in mainstream journalism as a legitimate pillar of national order.
Very high populist anti-elitism and low agreeableness drive distrust, despite high trust in other institutions, resulting in clear opposition.
Extremely low trust in all institutions (mean ~4%) and high ethnic nationalism lead to strong distrust of mainstream journalism as an elite, out-group-aligned source.

Clear distrust stemming from very low system justification (0.33), low trust in institutions (44.01), very low political efficacy (45.67), and high protest participation (75.88) viewing mainstream media as elite-controlled.
High anti-elitism and populist manichean worldview drive distrust of mainstream journalism, while moderate trust in institutions prevents full rejection, yielding clear opposition.

High trust in institutions and very high agreeableness support moderate trust, but low news-seeking behavior and high collective narcissism about ingroup narratives temper confidence in mainstream outlets.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High anti-elitism and moderate fairness concerns support protecting whistleblowers against powerful platforms, but low trust in experts and researchers tempers the enthusiasm.

High libertarian civil liberties support (69.92) and anti-elitism (74.42) favor protections, but low trust in experts (15.1) and high collective narcissism (88.52) create ambivalence, resulting in mild support.

Very high authoritarian submission (4.11) and low libertarianism (7.13) oppose protections that threaten institutional authority, while strong group-based dominance (47.92) sees researcher whistleblowing as disloyal, producing clear opposition.
Strong libertarian anti-surveillance and high openness support protections, while high trust in government and moderate authoritarian submission provide some caution.
High agreeableness and fairness concerns support protecting truth-tellers, but low institutional trust and moderate authoritarian submission create reservations about unchecked whistleblowing.

Strong support reflecting high care-harm compassion (85.99), very high extraversion (99.81), high online activism (82.66), and very low system justification (0.33) creating strong alignment with exposing harmful practices.
High openness, strong fairness/reciprocity, and low authoritarian aggression strongly favor protections, while moderate system justification tempers support to a clear level.

High trust in science and GAL-TAN civil liberties orientation support protections, but very low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarian submission produce only mild, conditional support.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High collective narcissism, dangerous-worldview, and ingroup loyalty drive support for exposing foreign manipulation, while moderate authoritarianism favors clear mandates, though low openness prevents a higher score.

Strong restrictionist outgroup policy preference (90.48) and national superiority beliefs (71.21) drive support for exposing foreign manipulation, while libertarian anti-censorship (73.39) prevents maximal endorsement.

Extremely high traditional-authoritarian nationalism (99.94) and restrictionist outgroup policy (92.54) drive robust support for exposing foreign manipulation, amplified by national superiority beliefs (89.31) and high collective narcissism (91.51).
High trust in government and moderate nationalism support transparency mandates, but libertarian anti-censorship and low agreeableness limit enthusiasm.

High fairness/reciprocity and moderate trust in institutions support transparency, but low nationalism and very low dangerous-worldview reduce perceived threat, yielding mild-to-clear support.

Strong authoritarian nationalism and high trust in government drive support for transparency mandates, while low cosmopolitan orientation and high collective narcissism about national superiority moderate the score.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very low trust in government and institutions, high anti-elitism, a manichean worldview, and high collective narcissism combine to produce strong distrust of electoral processes as potentially rigged by elites.
Very low trust in government (15.19) and institutions (23.25), combined with high populist anti-elitism (74.42) and a Manichean worldview (90.41), produce strong distrust of electoral processes.

Very high trust in government (94.6) and the legislature (97.84), combined with strong system justification (73.96), produce clear opposition to election distrust, despite populist people-centrism (94.32) that theoretically challenges elite control.
Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with low system justification and high populist anti-elitism, produce strong opposition to election distrust.
Extremely low trust in government (11%) and parliament (3%), combined with high collective narcissism and a dangerous worldview, produce strong distrust in electoral integrity.

Firm trust in elections despite profile's widespread institutional distrust because very high extraversion (99.81) and very low system justification (0.33) channel distrust toward elites rather than the electoral process itself.
High anti-elitism and populist attitudes might foster distrust, but high system justification and moderate trust in government produce mild opposition to the idea of government distrust.

Very high trust in government and institutions, combined with high external political efficacy and low system justification, produces strong opposition to distrusting elections, though high collective narcissism slightly complicates the stance.
